hook: The original East-Berlin currywurst stand, under the U2 viaduct since 1930
facts: Founded by Max Konnopke on 4 October 1930 on Schönhauser Allee.
Sits beneath the U2 elevated railway bridge, directly south of U-Bahnhof Eberswalder Straße.
Son Günter Konnopke began serving Currywurst here in 1960; it is regarded as the first snack stand to sell Currywurst in East Berlin.
Daughter Waltraud Ziervogel took over in 1976; the wooden booth was replaced by a metal stand in 1983.
During the 2010 U2 renovation the stand was relocated and rebuilt in 1960s style at the same spot, reopening in 2011.
Remained a family business operating continuously through the GDR era.
legend: Long held up as proof that the best Currywurst in Berlin came from the East, not the West.

sources.note: History (1930 founding, 1960 Currywurst, 2010-11 rebuild) and decimal coordinates 52.540486, 13.412176
Official site corroborating 1930 founding and Schönhauser Allee location

w: The original East-Berlin currywurst stand, under the U2 viaduct at Eberswalder Straße since 1930 — and the GDR's first to sell the sauce-drowned sausage, from 1960. Four generations of one family kept it going straight through the communist years. Rebuilt 1960s-style in 2011, same spot. The East's currywurst, and proud of it.

hook: Ralf Rüller's gallery-district roastery that pioneered third-wave coffee in Germany
facts: Founded by Ralf Rüller in 2010; the original cafe opened on Auguststraße in Berlin-Mitte's gallery district.
Current address Auguststraße 58, 10119 Berlin.
Rüller moved from London to Berlin in 2008, inspired by Borough Market, and opened The Barn after finding no proper specialty coffee in the city.
The dedicated roastery opened in September 2012.
Widely cited as one of Europe's leading specialty roasters and a pioneer of Germany's third-wave coffee movement.
Known historically for a no-laptops, no-distractions house style focused on the coffee.

sources.note: Official: Auguststraße 58, 10119 Berlin Mitte location, est. 2010
OSM geocode of Auguststraße 58 corroborating coordinates 52.5274, 13.3983
Founder Ralf Rüller, 2008 move from London, third-wave pioneer status

w: Ralf Rüller came back from London in 2008, found Berlin had no real specialty coffee, and opened The Barn on Auguststraße to fix it — roasting since 2012, now one of Europe's most exacting. The house rule was famously strict: no laptops, no distractions, just the coffee. Third-wave Germany started here. Put your phone away.

hook: Kreuzberg's legendary punk club, Germany's answer to CBGB, on Oranienstraße since 1978
facts: Music club on Oranienstraße (No. 190) near Heinrichplatz, named after the historic SO36 (Südost 36) postal code.
Opened 11-12 August 1978 with an ironic 'Mauerbaufestival'.
Hosted early German punk concerts; Iggy Pop, David Bowie and Einstürzende Neubauten are associated with its scene.
Bands including Die Ärzte, Die Toten Hosen, Slime and the Dead Kennedys played here.
Run for years by Turkish social worker Hilal Kurutan, it was a centre of the German punk and new wave scene until 1983.
Often described as Germany's answer to New York's CBGB.
legend: Long the spiritual home of Berlin punk and a cornerstone of Kreuzberg's subculture; today also hosts the queer 'Gayhane' night.

sources.note: History (1978 opening, postcode origin, bands) and coordinates 52.50028, 13.42222
Corroborates Oranienstraße/Kreuzberg location and cultural significance

w: Germany's answer to CBGB — a Kreuzberg punk club on Oranienstraße since 1978, named for the old postal code and opened with an 'ironic wall-building festival.' The Dead Kennedys and Die Ärzte played here; Bowie and Iggy haunted the scene. Still going, queer Gayhane nights and all. Loud since '78.

hook: A tiny, reservation-only Neukölln natural-wine bistro at the heart of the scene
facts: Located at Weichselstraße 7, 12043 Berlin (Neukölln).
Serves over 200 natural wines, mostly from French growers working close to nature, alongside local organic food.
Has only a few seats, so reservations are effectively required.
Repeatedly cited as a favourite of Berlin's natural-wine community since opening in the 2010s.
Pairs the wines with seasonal, regionally inspired small plates.

sources.note: Official site confirming natural-wine focus and Neukölln location

w: A tiny natural-wine bistro on a Neukölln corner, just a few seats and a reservation you'll need — 200-plus low-intervention bottles, mostly from French growers working close to nature, with seasonal small plates to match. A fixture of Berlin's natural-wine crowd since the 2010s. Book ahead, then settle in. Raw wine, Neukölln-style.

hook: The techno-defining record shop, founded 1989, now inside Kraftwerk beside Tresor
facts: Founded in 1989 by musician Mark Ernestus; specialist in electronic music and a pioneer of Berlin's techno scene.
After roughly three decades on Paul-Lincke-Ufer, it moved in 2023 to Köpenicker Straße 70, inside Kraftwerk Berlin alongside the Tresor club.
Stock centres on club-ready 12-inch techno plus house classics from Chicago and Detroit, reggae, dub and dubstep.
Part of the Berlin-Detroit axis that drove the 1990s techno exchange between the two cities.
DJs from Marcel Dettmann to Cassy to DJ Hell have worked behind its counter.
legend: Spoken of in the same breath as Tresor as a foundational institution of Berlin techno.

sources.note: 1989 founding by Mark Ernestus, 2023 move to Köpenicker Str. 70 / Kraftwerk, coordinates 52.49667, 13.42083
Corroborates current Köpenicker Straße location and staff/curation legend

w: The record shop that defined Berlin techno — Mark Ernestus opened it in 1989, and the Berlin-Detroit 12-inches that crossed its counter shaped the 1990s. Dettmann and DJ Hell have worked the till. In 2023 it moved into Kraftwerk, beside Tresor. House and dub and dubstep too, but techno runs deepest. Where the bass got its passport.

hook: Berlin's oldest public park, with a Grimm fairy-tale fountain and a hidden flak-tower hill
facts: Berlin's oldest public park at 52 hectares; the city council resolved to build it in 1840, with the oldest sections laid out 1846-48.
Conceived from an idea by landscape architect Peter Joseph Lenné, with planting by Gustav Meyer.
The Märchenbrunnen ('Fairy-Tale Fountain'), completed in 1913, has 106 stone figures drawn from the Brothers Grimm.
Contains 'Mont Klamott' (Großer Bunkerberg), a ~78 m hill of WWII rubble heaped over a destroyed Nazi-era flak bunker.
In 1848 the Cemetery of the March Fallen (Friedhof der Märzgefallenen) was established within the park.
Straddles the Friedrichshain and Prenzlauer Berg districts.
legend: Generations of Berlin children's lore attaches to the Märchenbrunnen; Mont Klamott lent its name to a Silly/Wolfgang Hilbig song about the buried war.

sources.note: Oldest park, 1840 decision, 1913 Märchenbrunnen, Mont Klamott flak-tower hill, coordinates 52.52667, 13.43389
Official Berlin.de corroboration of park history and features

w: Berlin's oldest public park, laid out from 1846 on a Lenné idea — and stranger than it looks. The 1913 Märchenbrunnen spills down in 106 stone Brothers Grimm characters; up the path rises Mont Klamott, a 78-metre hill of war rubble heaped over a buried Nazi flak bunker. History, gone green.

hook: The last of Berlin's ~900 ballrooms, dancing without interruption since 1913
facts: Ballroom, saloon and restaurant at Auguststraße 24, 10117 Berlin, in the historic Spandauer Vorstadt (Mitte).
Opened 13 September 1913 by Fritz and Clara Bühler as 'Bühler's Ballhaus'; later renamed for Clara ('Clärchen').
The only survivor of the roughly 900 ballrooms Berlin once had.
Survived two world wars and five systems of government while staying open for business.
Has protected status as a Berlin cultural monument (Kulturdenkmal).
Upstairs features a faded, partly mirrored 'Spiegelsaal' (mirror hall).
legend: Romanticised as a time-capsule of pre-war Berlin nightlife; featured on screen including in 'Inglourious Basterds' and 'Babylon Berlin'-era nostalgia.

sources.note: 1913 founding, last of ~900 ballrooms, Kulturdenkmal status, Auguststraße 24 and coordinates 52.526944, 13.396667
Corroborates history and Mitte / Auguststraße location

w: The last of the roughly 900 ballrooms Berlin once had, dancing without a break since 1913 — through two wars and five governments. Fritz and Clara Bühler opened it; the place took its name from Clara. Upstairs, a faded, half-mirrored Spiegelsaal looks straight out of pre-war Berlin. Tarantino filmed here. Take a turn on the floor.

hook: The 'Palace of Tears' departure hall where divided Berliners said goodbye, 1962-1989
facts: A steel-and-glass border-crossing pavilion at Berlin Friedrichstraße station, in operation 1962-1990.
Used only for westbound border crossings out of the GDR, with separate channels for West Berliners, foreigners, diplomats and East Germans.
Nicknamed 'Tränenpalast' (Palace of Tears) for the tearful farewells between East and West relatives.
Now a free museum on Cold War division and German reunification, opened 14 September 2011.
Run as part of the Stiftung Haus der Geschichte's exhibition network.
legend: Its name became shorthand for the human cost of the divided city.

sources.note: 1962-1990 border crossing, Palace of Tears name, 2011 museum, coordinates 52.5208, 13.3869
Official Berlin.de corroboration of Friedrichstraße location, history and free museum

w: The Palace of Tears — a glass-and-steel hall at Friedrichstraße where, from 1962 to 1989, divided Berliners said goodbye at the border between East and West. West-bound crossings only, sorted into channels by passport; the tears were on the platform behind. Now a free museum of the divided city. The name says everything.
